prompt: 2026 CANONICAL taxonomies for AI/agent security that an enterprise security review demands coverage of. Enumerate EXACTLY with IDs: (1) OWASP Top 10 for LLM Applications 2025 (LLM01..LLM10 with names). (2) OWASP Agentic AI / Agentic Security Initiative (ASI) threats / the agentic top threats list (T1..Tn with names). (3) MITRE ATLAS tactics+techniques relevant to LLMs/agents. (4) NIST AI RMF function families and ISO 42001 control areas and EU AI Act Article references that map to AI security. (5) The concrete checks an enterprise buyer's security questionnaire NOW asks specifically about a vendor's AI/agent (prompt injection resistance, data egress, tool/permission scoping, logging/auditability, human-in-the-loop, model provenance, PII handling). Cite official sources + dates.
content:
**1. OWASP Top 10 for LLM Applications 2025 (LLM01–LLM10)**  
Official source: OWASP GenAI Security Project / genai.owasp.org (2025 update, with pages dated/referenced through 2026 crawls).[[1]](https://genai.owasp.org/llm-top-10/)[[2]](https://trydeepteam.com/docs/frameworks-owasp-top-10-for-llms)

- **LLM01:2025 Prompt Injection** — Adversary-supplied instructions override intended behavior.
- **LLM02:2025 Sensitive Information Disclosure** — Secrets, PII, or confidential data leak through outputs or traces.
- **LLM03:2025 Supply Chain** — Compromised models, datasets, libraries, or hosting providers.
- **LLM04:2025 Data and Model Poisoning** — Malicious training, fine-tuning, or retrieval data shapes model behavior.
- **LLM05:2025 Improper Output Handling** — Downstream systems trust model output without validation.
- **LLM06:2025 Excessive Agency** — Model or agent holds more capability, autonomy, or permission than safe.
- **LLM07:2025 System Prompt Leakage** — Internal prompts, policies, or tool schemas are exfiltrated.
- **LLM08:2025 Vector and Embedding Weaknesses** — RAG indexing, retrieval, and embeddings introduce attack and leakage paths.
- **LLM09/10:2025** (commonly including **Unbounded Consumption** / resource abuse and related categories such as model extraction or additional agency/output risks per full project listings).

**2. OWASP Agentic AI / Agentic Security Initiative (ASI) threats (Top 10 for Agentic Applications 2026, ASI01–ASI10 / T1–Tn)**  
Official source: OWASP GenAI Security Project / genai.owasp.org (Top 10 for Agentic Applications for 2026, published December 9, 2025; ASI initiative resources 2026).[[3]](https://genai.owasp.org/resource/owasp-top-10-for-agentic-applications-for-2026/)[[4]](https://genai.owasp.org/resource/agentic-ai-threats-and-mitigations/)

Key enumerated threats (from project and expert summaries; full list is peer-reviewed and downloadable via the site):
- **ASI01 / T1: Agent Goal Hijack** — Treating natural-language inputs as untrusted; goal manipulation via prompts/files/RAG/other agents.
- **ASI02 / T2: Tool Misuse & Exploitation** — Unauthorized or malicious tool invocation and permission abuse.
- Additional high-priority agentic risks typically include memory/context poisoning, tool/data poisoning, excessive autonomy, goal misalignment, and related categories (full T1–T10 framework covers planning/acting/decision workflows, with mitigations and crosswalks to LLM Top 10).

**3. MITRE ATLAS tactics + techniques relevant to LLMs/agents**  
Official source: atlas.mitre.org (living knowledge base; v5.1+ updates through 2025–2026 emphasize agentic/GenAI).[[5]](https://atlas.mitre.org/)[[6]](https://www.vectra.ai/topics/mitre-atlas)

- **16 core tactics** (AML.TAxxxx): Reconnaissance, Resource Development, Initial Access, AI Model Access, Execution, Persistence, Privilege Escalation, Defense Evasion, Credential Access, Discovery, Lateral Movement, Collection, AI Attack Staging, Command and Control (added ~2025), Exfiltration, Impact.
- **Relevant techniques** (examples; 80+ total, many LLM/agent-specific): AML.T0051 (LLM Prompt Injection — direct/indirect/triggered), AML.T0020 (Poison Training Data), AML.T0080 (AI Agent Context Poisoning — memory/thread), AML.T0110 (AI Agent Tool Poisoning), AML.T0053 (AI Agent Tool Invocation), AML.T0086 (Exfiltration via AI Agent Tool Invocation), AML.T0043 (Craft Adversarial Data), AML.T0054 (LLM Jailbreak), AML.T0070 (RAG Poisoning), AML.T0093 (Prompt Infiltration via Public-Facing Application), plus supply-chain, model manipulation, and resource-consumption variants.

**4. NIST AI RMF function families; ISO 42001 control areas; EU AI Act Article references mapping to AI security**  
- **NIST AI RMF (AI RMF 1.0, 2023; ongoing profiles 2026)**: Four core function families — **Govern** (culture, policies, accountability), **Map** (context and risk identification), **Measure** (metrics, testing, evaluation), **Manage** (mitigation, monitoring, response).[[7]](https://orca.security/resources/blog/nist-ai-risk-management-framework-ai-rmf/)[[8]](https://nvlpubs.nist.gov/nistpubs/ai/nist.ai.100-1.pdf)
- **ISO/IEC 42001:2023 (AI management systems)**: Annex A controls organized into nine areas — Internal organization (A.3), Resources for AI systems (A.4), Assessing impacts of AI systems (A.5), AI system life cycle (A.6), Data for AI systems (A.7), Information for interested parties (A.8), Use of AI systems (A.9), Third-party/customer relationships (A.10), plus overarching policies and risk processes (38 controls total, emphasizing security, robustness, data governance, human oversight).[[9]](https://www.vanta.com/collection/iso-42001/iso-42001-controls)
- **EU AI Act (2024 regulation, applicable references)**: Article 9 (risk management system throughout lifecycle), Article 10 (data governance/quality), Article 13 (transparency/info to deployers), Article 14 (human oversight), Article 15 (accuracy, robustness, cybersecurity — explicit security requirements), plus high-risk obligations (Articles 8–17) and prohibited/unacceptable-risk rules (Article 5).[[10]](https://artificialintelligenceact.eu/high-level-summary/)

**5. Concrete checks enterprise buyer security questionnaires now ask about a vendor’s AI/agent**  
These map directly to the enumerated items (prompt injection resistance, data egress, tool/permission scoping, logging/auditability, human-in-the-loop, model provenance, PII handling) and are standard in 2025–2026 assessments. Sources include vendor questionnaire analyses and best-practice guides.[[11]](https://www.atlassystems.com/blog/ai-vendor-risk-questionnaire)[[12]](https://delve.co/blog/what-security-questionnaires-should-ask-ai-vendors)

- **Prompt injection resistance**: Input validation/sanitization, sandboxing, schema enforcement, monitoring for anomalous prompts, defense against direct/indirect/triggered injections.
- **Data egress controls**: Egress filtering, outbound request restrictions, segmentation, prevention of exfiltration via outputs/tools/RAG.
- **Tool/permission scoping**: Least-privilege tool access, permission boundaries, isolation of agent capabilities, prevention of tool misuse/exploitation.
- **Logging/auditability**: Comprehensive logging of prompts, tool calls, decisions, outputs; audit trails for traceability and incident response.
- **Human-in-the-loop (HITL)**: Mandatory approval gates for high-impact actions, oversight mechanisms, automation bias controls.
- **Model provenance**: Supply-chain transparency, model/dataset origin verification, integrity checks against poisoning/backdoors.
- **PII handling**: Data minimization, anonymization/redaction, consent/opt-out for training/use, compliance with privacy rules, leakage prevention.

These taxonomies are the canonical references demanded in enterprise reviews as of mid-2026. Always verify the latest versions directly on the official sites (OWASP GenAI, MITRE ATLAS, NIST, ISO, EU AI Act portal) as frameworks evolve.
